content.text: occurs when a language model declines to complete a request — either because it judges the request unsafe, outside its capabilities, or structurally ambiguous. A
is the pre-planned alternative behaviour your system executes when the primary path fails: the model refuses, returns output that cannot be parsed, times out, or hits an API error.

Together, refusal handling and fallback design form the reliability layer of any agentic system. A well-prompted pipeline anticipates that the model will occasionally not cooperate and builds explicit code paths for each failure mode. Without this layer, a single refused request can silently break a long-running agent loop, corrupt downstream state, or silently skip work the user expected to be done.
Refusals come in several distinct varieties. A
means the model believes the request violates its content policies — harmful content, explicit material, or activity that appears illegal. A
means the model believes it cannot reliably complete the task — "I don't have access to real-time data", "I cannot browse the internet". A
means the model produced text but not the JSON, XML, or structured schema the caller required. An
is when the model answers but hedges so strongly that the output is not actionable.

Production agentic systems make thousands of LLM calls per day. Even a refusal rate of 0.5% means a system calling the API 10 000 times per day encounters 50 unhandled failures. If those failures silently propagate — an empty string stored as an article summary, a missing action in a task queue — they corrupt downstream data and user trust in ways that are hard to diagnose later.

Refusals are also not uniformly distributed. They cluster on edge cases: requests that contain unusual terminology, tasks at the boundary of the model's stated capabilities, prompts that inadvertently use phrasing the model associates with harmful intent. An agent processing diverse user input must be especially robust, because it cannot control the content of what it processes.

Fallback design matters equally. The question is not just "what do we do when the model refuses?" but "what is the right degraded behaviour?" For some tasks, a structured fallback to a simpler prompt is correct. For others, the right answer is to escalate to a human reviewer. Choosing the wrong fallback can be worse than no fallback: returning stale cached data when freshness is required, or silently skipping a moderation step because the model refused to run it.
Systems with explicit fallback paths continue functioning through transient model misbehaviour rather than crashing or silently corrupting data.
A fallback that returns a degraded-but-correct result within a known time budget is more useful than a primary path that sometimes takes 30 seconds before refusing.
Prompt redesign techniques — adding context, rephrasing, providing examples — convert many refusals into successful completions, improving throughput without changing the underlying model.
Logging refusal events with the triggering prompt and context gives operators data to identify systemic prompt problems rather than discovering failures through downstream symptoms.
Users experience a sensible informative message rather than an application crash or a silent empty result when the model cannot help.
Treating every model output as potentially malformed forces validation code that also defends against prompt-injection attacks and jailbroken outputs.
The primary cost of robust fallback handling is
. Every fallback path is additional code to write, test, and maintain. If a fallback triggers another LLM call, it adds latency and cost. If a fallback returns static data, it may become stale.
A retry loop adds the full API roundtrip to the request duration. Three retries with exponential backoff can turn a 2-second call into a 20-second one in the worst case.
Retry attempts consume tokens. In a high-volume system, an elevated refusal rate directly inflates the API bill.
A fallback that silently succeeds on a rephrased prompt may hide the fact that the original prompt was systematically flawed. Always log which path executed.
A primary result and a fallback result may differ in quality or format. Downstream systems must handle both shapes, or the fallback must normalize its output to match the primary contract.
A fallback that re-attempts with the same prompt on a safety refusal will refuse again. Fallback logic must distinguish transient errors from permanent refusals and not retry the latter blindly.

A reliable prompting pipeline treats the LLM call as an operation that can succeed in one of three ways — structured output, degraded output, or escalation — and fail in two ways — transient error or permanent refusal. The code around the LLM call classifies the response and routes to the appropriate handler.

Response classification happens in two stages. First, the API response itself is inspected: HTTP errors (429 rate limit, 500 server error, timeout) are transient and retryable. Second, the model's text output is inspected: does it contain the expected JSON schema? Does it start with a refusal phrase ("I'm sorry", "I cannot", "I'm unable")? Does it answer the question at all?
When a refusal is detected, the fallback strategy depends on the refusal type. A
should not be retried with the same prompt — it will refuse again. Instead, either rephrase to avoid triggering phrasing, or escalate to a human. A
often means the prompt asked the model to do something outside its scope; the fallback may be a simpler decomposed version of the task. A
(the model answered in prose when JSON was required) warrants a corrective follow-up prompt that includes the failed output and asks the model to reformat it.

Transient API errors use standard retry logic: exponential backoff with jitter, a maximum retry count, and a circuit breaker that stops retrying if the error rate exceeds a threshold. This prevents a retry storm from multiplying load on an already-degraded API endpoint.
The pipeline has four nodes. The
assembles the system prompt and user context, including any rephrase adaptations made by the fallback loop. The

validates and parses the result. On success (green), the structured output flows to the caller. On refusal (amber), the fallback loop rephrases the prompt and re-enters the Prompt Builder. On error (red), exponential backoff retries directly into the LLM API node. Both fallback loops are bounded by a maximum attempt counter to prevent infinite cycles.
Explicit refusal and fallback handling is needed in every production LLM system. The decision is not whether to implement it, but how deep to go at each layer.
for any API call that might hit a rate limit (HTTP 429) or a transient server error (HTTP 500, 503). This is table stakes for any production system.

when your system processes untrusted or diverse user input that might inadvertently trigger safety filters — content moderation agents, document processing pipelines, or agents that accept free-form task descriptions.
and a corrective follow-up prompt when the task requires machine-readable output (JSON, XML, specific schema) that the model might occasionally produce in the wrong format.
as the final fallback in workflows where a wrong automated answer is worse than no answer — compliance, medical information, legal summaries.
(decomposed, reduced context) when the primary task is complex and the model sometimes fails on length or complexity rather than safety.
for internal development tooling or one-off batch jobs where a failure can simply be retried manually and the cost of the fallback code outweighs the benefit.
Wrap every LLM call in a function that catches API exceptions and implements retry with exponential backoff. Use a library like
rather than writing retry loops by hand.
After receiving a response, classify it: check the HTTP status, then check
(OpenAI), then inspect the text for refusal patterns.
that maps text patterns to refusal types. Use simple keyword matching first; upgrade to a small LLM classifier only if false-positive rate becomes a problem.
For each refusal type, implement a
function: rephrase for safety refusals, simplify or decompose for capability refusals, correct for format refusals.
Cap fallback loops at a maximum attempt count (typically 2-3). After exhaustion, escalate: write to a dead-letter queue, log a structured event, or return a known error code to the caller.
Log every refusal event with the original prompt (or a hash thereof), the detected refusal type, the fallback strategy applied, and whether it succeeded. This data drives future prompt improvements.
Add output schema validation using Pydantic or JSON Schema to catch format failures separately from semantic refusals.

# Retry transient API errors with tenacity
# pip install anthropic tenacity

import anthropic
from tenacity import (
    retry,
    stop_after_attempt,
    wait_exponential,
    retry_if_exception_type,
    before_sleep_log,
)
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Exceptions that are safe to retry
RETRYABLE = (
    anthropic.RateLimitError,
    anthropic.APIStatusError,  # covers 500/503
    anthropic.APIConnectionError,
    anthropic.APITimeoutError,
)

@retry(
    retry=retry_if_exception_type(RETRYABLE),
    wait=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, min=2, max=30),
    stop=stop_after_attempt(4),
    before_sleep=before_sleep_log(logger, logging.WARNING),
    reraise=True,
)
def call_llm(messages: list[dict], system: str = "") -> anthropic.types.Message:
    """Call the Anthropic API with automatic retry on transient errors."""
    kwargs: dict = {
        "model": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        "max_tokens": 1024,
        "messages": messages,
    }
    if system:
        kwargs["system"] = system
    return client.messages.create(**kwargs)

# Usage
try:
    response = call_llm([{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise this document."}])
    print(response.content[0].text)
except RETRYABLE as exc:
    # All retries exhausted — escalate
    logger.error("LLM call failed after retries: %s", exc)
    raise

# Validate JSON output and issue a corrective follow-up on format failure
import json
from typing import Any
import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class SentimentResult(BaseModel):
    sentiment: str   # "positive" | "negative" | "neutral"
    confidence: float  # 0.0 – 1.0
    explanation: str

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You are a sentiment analyser. Respond ONLY with a JSON object matching this schema:
{"sentiment": "positive|negative|neutral", "confidence": 0.0-1.0, "explanation": "..."}
Do not include any text before or after the JSON.
"""

CORRECTIVE_TEMPLATE = """
Your previous response could not be parsed as JSON:
---
{bad_output}
---
Please respond ONLY with the JSON object and nothing else.
"""

def analyse_sentiment(text: str) -> SentimentResult | None:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": f"Analyse: {text}"}]

    for attempt in range(3):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
            max_tokens=256,
            system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
            messages=messages,
        )
        raw = response.content[0].text.strip()

        # Strip optional markdown code fences
        if raw.startswith("```"):
            raw = raw.split("\n", 1)[1].rsplit("```", 1)[0].strip()

        try:
            parsed: Any = json.loads(raw)
            return SentimentResult(**parsed)
        except (json.JSONDecodeError, ValidationError) as exc:
            if attempt < 2:
                # Feed the bad output back and ask for correction
                messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": raw})
                messages.append({
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": CORRECTIVE_TEMPLATE.format(bad_output=raw),
                })
            else:
                print(f"Format fallback exhausted after {attempt+1} attempts: {exc}")
                return None

result = analyse_sentiment("The new API is incredibly fast and easy to use!")
if result:
    print(f"Sentiment: {result.sentiment} ({result.confidence:.0%}) — {result.explanation}")

A document intelligence service processes thousands of uploaded PDFs per day, extracting structured metadata: document type, date, named parties, and a one-sentence summary. The pipeline uses a three-layer fallback strategy developed after observing two months of production behaviour.
: the tenacity-based retry wrapper handles HTTP 429 and 500 errors transparently, with a 2s/4s/8s backoff. This covers roughly 0.3% of calls.

: the primary prompt asks for a JSON object. If Pydantic validation fails, a corrective follow-up prompt is sent with the malformed output. This converts about 85% of format failures into valid results. The remaining 15% are logged to a dead-letter queue for nightly batch reprocessing with a simplified prompt.

: some documents contain legal language that occasionally triggers safety filters (contracts mentioning criminal penalties, medical consent forms). These are detected using the refusal classifier and rerouted to a separate prompt that omits the document body and only processes the title and metadata header. If that also refuses, the job is marked for human review.

The combined effect: 99.7% of documents receive a structured result automatically within the SLA. 0.2% are handled by the fallback prompts. 0.1% go to human review. Without the fallback layers, the human review queue would be approximately 25 times larger.
Think of an LLM call as a
vending machine that sometimes rejects your money

. Most of the time you put in a coin and get a snack. Occasionally the coin is slightly bent and the machine ejects it — not because you did anything wrong, but because the sensor was calibrated for perfect coins. Occasionally the machine is out of stock. Occasionally it is temporarily out of order.

For a bent coin (format refusal), you straighten it slightly and try again — the corrective follow-up prompt. For out-of-stock (capability refusal), you pick something adjacent from the menu — the simplified or decomposed task. For out-of-order (transient API error), you wait a moment and try again — exponential backoff. And for a machine that simply will not accept your request no matter what you do (hard safety refusal), you walk to a different vending machine or find a person to help you — human escalation.

The key insight is that none of these outcomes are catastrophic if you planned for them. The vending machine does not owe you a snack; the LLM does not owe you a response. Your system owes the user a reliable experience, which requires anticipating all of the machine's possible non-answers in advance.

The most expensive mistake is retrying an identical prompt after a safety refusal. Safety refusals are not transient — the model will return the same refusal every time. Blind retry wastes tokens, adds latency, and does not fix the problem. Distinguish between transient API errors (HTTP 429/500, which should be retried) and semantic refusals (which need a different strategy).
Treating All Non-JSON as a Format Refusal
Sometimes the model produces valid JSON wrapped in a markdown code fence:

. This is not a refusal — it is a minor formatting deviation. Parse this correctly before declaring a failure. A simple strip of code fences resolves 40-60% of apparent format failures without any additional LLM call.

on an LLM call swallows authentication errors, invalid request errors, and bugs in your own code. Catch only the specific exception classes that represent retryable conditions. Let programming errors propagate normally.

A fallback that silently succeeds hides the fact that the primary prompt is failing. If 10% of calls are hitting the fallback path, that is a strong signal that the primary prompt needs to be redesigned — but only if you are logging the fallback event. Always record which path executed, the refusal type, and the original prompt (or a hash of it) so you can identify patterns.

A fallback that generates a new prompt and sends it back through the same pipeline can loop indefinitely if it keeps producing refusals. Always cap the attempt count at the top of the loop and ensure the exit path on exhaustion is deterministic — return an error, write to a queue, or escalate.
Identify whether a failure is a transient API error, a safety refusal, a capability refusal, or a format error before choosing the response. Each type has a different optimal strategy.
Anthropic's SDK raises specific exception classes (
). Catch each class separately and handle it correctly rather than catching the base
When many workers hit a rate limit simultaneously and all retry at the same time, the spike repeats. Add random jitter (
in tenacity) to spread the retry load.
Strip code fences before JSON parsing.
at the start before declaring a format failure. This alone eliminates a large fraction of false format errors.
Prefer guided rephrasing over raw rephrasing.

When a safety refusal fires, do not simply send the prompt with a different wording chosen at random. Use a fixed rephrase template that has been tested to reduce refusal rate on your specific task domain.
Set a hard timeout at the application layer.

Even with SDK-level timeouts, set an overall budget for the whole call-with-fallback cycle. If the entire operation (primary + retries + fallback) takes more than N seconds, abort and return an error rather than consuming the user's time.
Requests that exhaust all fallback strategies should go to a durable queue for later reprocessing or human review. Do not discard them silently.
Use mocked LLM responses that return refusal text or raise API exceptions to test each branch of your fallback code. Do not rely on triggering real refusals in tests.

Retry logic adds variable latency. In the worst case — three retries with exponential backoff — a request that would take 2 seconds on success takes 2 + 4 + 8 = 14 seconds before the final retry is sent, and 14 + 2 = 16 seconds if that retry also fails. Set a maximum total budget that is consistent with your user-facing SLA and abort early if the budget is exhausted.

At scale, a 0.5% refusal rate on 100,000 daily calls means 500 fallback invocations per day. Each fallback invocation that sends an additional LLM call doubles the cost for that 0.5%. This is usually acceptable. But at 5% refusal rate on 1,000,000 calls — 50,000 fallback calls — the cost becomes significant and the priority shifts to fixing the primary prompt.

When error rate exceeds a threshold (e.g. 20% of calls failing in a 60-second window), open the circuit: stop sending requests to the API for a short period and return errors immediately. This prevents a retry storm from compounding an API outage.

If a primary result is not strictly required synchronously — a content enrichment step, a background summariser — run it asynchronously and handle failures by scheduling a retry job rather than blocking the main request path.
Instrument your refusal detection and fallback invocation with counters broken down by refusal type. A sudden spike in
events is an early signal that a prompt changed or a new class of user input appeared.

Consider falling back to a smaller, cheaper model for the corrective attempt on format failures. If the primary model produced valid JSON 95% of the time and the corrective prompt is simple ("reformat this as JSON"), a smaller model can complete the correction at a fraction of the cost.
The Python retry library of choice. Provides
, and async support out of the box.
Validates structured LLM output against a schema. Raises
with field-level detail on the first mismatch, making it straightforward to build corrective prompts that reference the specific missing or wrong field.

A library that wraps the Anthropic and OpenAI clients to automatically request structured output and retry with corrective prompts when the output does not match the requested Pydantic model. Reduces boilerplate for the format-fallback pattern significantly.
Provide built-in retry mechanisms and output parsers with fix-up prompts. The
wraps any output parser and issues a corrective LLM call on parse failure.
Includes built-in automatic retry for rate-limit errors (configurable via
parameter on the client constructor). The default is 2 retries; setting it to 0 disables automatic retry so you can implement your own logic.

Instrument refusal type counters and fallback invocation rates. A refusal dashboard provides early warning of prompt regressions and helps prioritise which refusal types to address in the next prompt iteration.
Requests that exhaust all fallback strategies should be written to a durable queue rather than discarded. Consumer workers can reprocess them with adjusted prompts or route them to human review.

These strategies layer. A typical production implementation uses all of them: backoff for transient errors, corrective prompts for format failures, rephrase for over-refusals, simpler prompts for capability limits, and human escalation as the final backstop. The circuit breaker sits above all of them and sheds load during sustained outages.

Refusal handling interacts directly with security. A system that automatically rephrases prompts to avoid safety filters may inadvertently bypass legitimate guardrails, not just over-eager ones. The rephrase strategy must be designed conservatively: add context that legitimises the request, do not strip context that the model correctly identified as problematic.
Do not treat all safety refusals as over-refusals.
Some requests that the model refuses genuinely should be refused. Inspect the refusal reason before deciding to rephrase. Log every rephrasing attempt for human audit.
Validate fallback output as rigorously as primary output.
A fallback call uses a different prompt and may return output in a different format or with different confidence. Apply the same Pydantic schema validation to both paths.
Guard against prompt injection in fallback prompts.

If the corrective prompt includes the model's previous (malformed) output, an attacker who can influence the model's output could inject instructions into the corrective prompt. Sanitise or truncate the previous output before embedding it.

In regulated industries, which fallback path executed for which request may be material to compliance reporting. Treat refusal event logs with the same retention and access-control requirements as other operational logs.

A sudden increase in safety refusals may indicate that user input has shifted (new use-case, abuse campaign) or that a prompt was inadvertently changed. Alert at 2x the baseline refusal rate and investigate promptly.
Refusals are not uniform: safety, capability, format, and uncertainty-driven non-answers each require a different fallback strategy. Detecting the type before acting is the first step.
Transient API errors (rate limits, server errors) should be retried with exponential backoff and jitter. Safety refusals should not be retried identically — they will refuse again.
The corrective follow-up prompt pattern resolves 70-90% of format failures by feeding the malformed output back to the model with an explicit instruction to reformat it.
Always cap fallback loops at a maximum attempt count and define the exit path on exhaustion: dead-letter queue, structured error return, or human escalation.
Log every refusal event with the refusal type, the fallback applied, and whether it succeeded. Without this data, you cannot distinguish a well-functioning fallback from a broken primary prompt.
Strip markdown code fences before JSON parsing. This resolves the majority of apparent format failures without an additional LLM call.
Security and fallback interact: rephrase fallbacks that bypass safety filters must be audited. Corrective prompts that include previous LLM output must be sanitised against prompt injection.
In high-volume systems, add a circuit breaker above the retry logic to prevent a retry storm from compounding an API outage.
